description: System administration and network configuration session

messages.content: Need help setting up a VPN between our office and AWS VPC. Currently using WireGuard.

WireGuard for site-to-site VPN is a great choice — simpler than IPSec, performant, and easy to maintain. Are you connecting from a static IP office? And which AWS region is your VPC in? Do you want a managed solution (AWS VPN) or self-managed (EC2 running WireGuard)?
Static IP office, us-west-2 region. Want self-managed WireGuard on EC2 for cost savings. Office CIDR: 10.0.0.0/24, VPC CIDR: 172.31.0.0/16.

Perfect setup for self-managed WireGuard. Here's the plan:
1. Launch EC2 t3.micro in public subnet, assign Elastic IP
2. Disable source/destination check on the EC2 instance
3. Install WireGuard: `apt install wireguard`
4. Configure routing on EC2 to forward traffic to VPC
5. Configure WireGuard on office router/server

I'll walk through each step.
EC2 is up with Elastic IP 10.0.0.X. Source/dest check disabled. WireGuard installed.

Great. Now configure WireGuard on the EC2 side. Create `/etc/wireguard/wg0.conf`:

```ini
[Interface]
Address = 10.200.0.1/24
ListenPort = 51820
PrivateKey = <ec2-private-key>
# Enable IP forwarding and NAT
PostUp = sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1; iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE
PostDown = iptables -D FORWARD -i wg0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE

[Peer]
PublicKey = <office-public-key>
AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.0/24
```

Generate keys: `wg genkey | tee privatekey | wg pubkey > publickey`
Keys generated. Office config set up similarly. When I run wg-quick up wg0, getting 'Address already in use'.

The 10.200.0.1/24 subnet might conflict with an existing interface. Check: `ip addr show` and `ip route show`. If wg0 is partially up: `wg-quick down wg0` then `wg-quick up wg0`. If 10.200.0.0/24 is actually in use on this system, change the WireGuard interface address to 10.200.1.0/24.
Found a docker0 bridge at 10.200.0.1. Changed WireGuard to 10.200.2.0/24. Now up!

Good catch! Docker commonly uses 10.200.x.x ranges. Now verify the tunnel:
```bash
# From EC2, ping office gateway:
ping 10.0.0.1 -c 4

# Check WireGuard status:
wg show
# Look for 'latest handshake' — if within last 2 min, tunnel is live
```
wg show shows handshake 30 seconds ago. But ping to 10.0.0.1 failing.

Handshake succeeds but routing fails. Check:
1. EC2 Security Group: allow UDP 51820 inbound from office IP, and ICMP for ping
2. VPC Route Table: add route `10.0.0.0/24 → EC2 instance ID`
3. Office firewall: allow traffic from 172.31.0.0/16
4. IP forwarding: `cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` should return 1

Also: `sudo tcpdump -i wg0 icmp` on EC2 — do you see packets arriving?
tcpdump shows ICMP arriving on wg0 but no reply. Found it: VPC route table missing the 10.0.0.0/24 route!

Classic! The VPC route table routes traffic INTO the VPC but needs to know to send 10.0.0.0/24 traffic BACK through your WireGuard EC2 instance. Add route: destination `10.0.0.0/24`, target `Instance ID of WireGuard EC2`.
Route added. Ping works both ways now! VPN tunnel is up.

Excellent! Enable WireGuard auto-start: `systemctl enable wg-quick@wg0`. Also set up monitoring — alert if the tunnel goes down. A simple cron: `* * * * * ping -c 1 10.0.0.1 || systemctl restart wg-quick@wg0` as a basic keepalive.
Done. Next topic: our Nginx is serving HTTP, need to add HTTPS with Let's Encrypt.

Certbot for Let's Encrypt with Nginx — straightforward:
```bash
# Install certbot
apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx

# Obtain and install certificate:
certbot --nginx -d example.com -d www.example.com

# Test auto-renewal:
certbot renew --dry-run
```

Certbot automatically modifies your Nginx config to add HTTPS and redirect HTTP→HTTPS. Certificates auto-renew via systemd timer.
Certbot ran successfully. But now getting 'too many redirects' in browser.

Redirect loop usually means your app thinks it's getting HTTP even though Nginx is serving HTTPS. If your app is behind a load balancer or proxy: set `X-Forwarded-Proto` header handling in your app. For Django: add `SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER = ('HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO', 'https')`. For Node.js/Express: `app.set('trust proxy', 1)`. Also check Nginx: you may have double redirect rules.
Found it — Nginx config had both Certbot's redirect AND my manual HTTP→HTTPS redirect. Removed duplicate. Fixed!

Classic double-redirect issue when Certbot edits an already-modified config. Test your SSL: `curl -I https://example.com` and check the SSL rating at ssllabs.com/ssltest. Add HSTS header for extra security:
```nginx
add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;
```
SSL Labs gives A rating. Added HSTS. Now: our syslog is growing too large. 50GB of logs.

50GB is manageable with proper log rotation. Check current logrotate config: `cat /etc/logrotate.d/syslog`. Standard fix:
```
/var/log/syslog {
    daily
    rotate 7
    compress
    delaycompress
    missingok
    notifempty
    postrotate
        /usr/lib/rsyslog/rsyslog-rotate
    endscript
}
```

Also: identify what's generating so much log. `sudo du -sh /var/log/*` and `sudo journalctl --disk-usage`.
Found source: a misconfigured app logging every request at DEBUG level in production. Fixed to INFO. Also applied logrotate.

Excellent root-cause fix. After changing log level, restart the app and verify: `tail -f /var/log/app.log` should show only INFO and above. Log volume should drop 90%+. Set up disk usage alerting: alert at 70% disk usage so you catch this earlier next time.
Will add disk monitoring. Our backup strategy is manual rsync. Should we automate this?

Yes, absolutely automate backups. Recommended stack:
1. **Database**: `pg_dump` or `mysqldump` → compress → S3 (use `aws s3 cp` or `rclone`)
2. **Files**: `restic` — deduplication, encryption, S3/B2 backend, excellent retention policies
3. **Schedule**: cron or systemd timers
4. **Test restores**: Monthly restore test to verify backups are valid
5. **3-2-1 rule**: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite (S3 is your offsite)

Restic example: `restic -r s3:s3.amazonaws.com/my-backups backup /data --password-file /etc/restic-password`
We use Postgres. Show me a complete backup script.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# postgres-backup.sh — Daily Postgres backup to S3

set -euo pipefail

DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d-%H%M)
DB_NAME="myapp"
BACKUP_DIR="/tmp/backups"
S3_BUCKET="s3://my-company-backups/postgres"

mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Dump all databases
pg_dumpall -U postgres | gzip > "$BACKUP_DIR/postgres-$DATE.sql.gz"

# Upload to S3
aws s3 cp "$BACKUP_DIR/postgres-$DATE.sql.gz" "$S3_BUCKET/postgres-$DATE.sql.gz"

# Verify upload
aws s3 ls "$S3_BUCKET/postgres-$DATE.sql.gz" || { echo 'Upload failed!'; exit 1; }

# Cleanup local backup
rm -f "$BACKUP_DIR/postgres-$DATE.sql.gz"

# Delete S3 backups older than 30 days
aws s3 ls "$S3_BUCKET/" | awk '{print $4}' | while read f; do
    fdate=$(echo "$f" | grep -oP '\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}' | head -1)
    if [[ $(date -d "$fdate" +%s 2>/dev/null) -lt $(date -d '30 days ago' +%s) ]]; then
        aws s3 rm "$S3_BUCKET/$f"
    fi
done

echo "Backup completed: postgres-$DATE.sql.gz"
```

Add to cron: `0 2 * * * /opt/scripts/postgres-backup.sh >> /var/log/backup.log 2>&1`
Perfect script. Will add monitoring for backup success/failure via email.

For backup monitoring, add to the script's error handler:
```bash
trap 'echo "Backup FAILED at $(date)" | mail -s "BACKUP FAILURE" ops@company.com' ERR
```

Or use a dead man's switch service like Healthchecks.io — your backup script pings a URL on success; if no ping within 25 hours, it alerts you. More reliable than error-only email alerts.
Great. We're also planning to move from bare metal to cloud. What's the migration checklist?

Bare metal to AWS migration checklist:

**Assessment:**
- [ ] Inventory all services (app servers, DBs, caches, file storage)
- [ ] Measure CPU/memory usage patterns over 4 weeks
- [ ] Identify compliance/data residency requirements
- [ ] Calculate TCO: bare metal vs AWS

**Pre-migration:**
- [ ] Set up AWS account with billing alerts
- [ ] Design VPC architecture (subnets, security groups, NAT)
- [ ] Set up VPN or Direct Connect for migration period
- [ ] Test application in AWS environment

**Migration:**
- [ ] Use AWS Database Migration Service for DB
- [ ] Use AWS Server Migration Service or CloudEndure for app servers
- [ ] Migrate in dependency order: DB first, then app, then frontend
- [ ] Keep bare metal live for 2 weeks post-migration as fallback

**Post-migration:**
- [ ] Optimize instance types (AWS Compute Optimizer)
- [ ] Set up AWS Cost Explorer
- [ ] Configure CloudWatch monitoring
- [ ] Decommission bare metal
Comprehensive checklist. Timeline estimate for 3 servers: app, DB, cache?

For 3-server stack (app + DB + cache):
- **Week 1-2**: AWS setup, VPC design, VPN connectivity
- **Week 3**: Launch AWS equivalents (EC2 for app/cache, RDS for DB)
- **Week 4**: Data sync setup (DMS for DB, rsync for files)
- **Week 5**: Testing in AWS, DNS cutover prep
- **Week 6**: DNS cutover (5min TTL), live validation
- **Week 7-8**: Monitoring period, bare metal on standby
- **Week 9**: Decommission bare metal

Total: ~2 months for a careful, low-risk migration.
Perfect timeline. Thanks for the comprehensive sysadmin session!

Great progress today! Highlights: WireGuard VPN operational, HTTPS with A-rated SSL, log volume fixed, automated Postgres backups to S3, and a clear cloud migration roadmap. Well-planned infrastructure work!
One more: SSH hardening best practices. We have password auth enabled.

SSH hardening checklist — edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`:
```
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no  # CRITICAL: disable password auth
PubkeyAuthentication yes
X11Forwarding no
MaxAuthTries 3
LoginGraceTime 60
AllowUsers deploy ops  # Whitelist specific users only
Port 2222  # Move off port 22 (reduces scan noise)
ClientAliveInterval 300
ClientAliveCountMax 2
```

Also install `fail2ban`: auto-bans IPs with too many failed SSH attempts.
Will apply all these. Our servers are currently accessible from any IP. Should we restrict SSH by IP?

Yes — restrict SSH access by IP as defense-in-depth:

**Option 1: Security Group** (AWS): Add inbound rule for port 2222 from only your office IP and VPN IP range. Easiest to manage.

**Option 2: fail2ban**: Not IP restriction but blocks brute-force attackers.

**Option 3: Port knocking**: Security through obscurity — only opens SSH port after a specific sequence of connection attempts. More complex.

Recommend Option 1 (Security Group IP restriction) + fail2ban as belt-and-suspenders.
We'll restrict via Security Group to office IP and VPN subnet (10.0.0.0/24). Thanks!

Perfect defense-in-depth setup: WireGuard VPN + Security Group IP restriction + SSH key auth + fail2ban. This is enterprise-grade access control. Remember to document emergency access procedures in case VPN goes down.
Good point about emergency access. We'll keep a break-glass procedure via AWS Systems Manager Session Manager.

AWS SSM Session Manager is exactly right for break-glass. No SSH ports needed — uses IAM auth. Add it to your runbook and test it quarterly. This is the professional way to handle emergency access without weakening your security posture.
All set. Comprehensive session. Thanks!
Excellent sysadmin work today! Your infrastructure is now significantly more secure and resilient. Good luck with the cloud migration!
